text: The robotic surgical system is used to open the porta hepatis, exposing the hepatic artery. Lymph nodes adjacent to the hepatic artery are excised and removed as specimens.

The surgeon cuts connections from the common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, and right hepatic artery to prevent extrahepatic perfusion from the pump. The gastroduodenal artery is encircled, and a branch off its right side is ligated using a suture to ensure it does not obstruct the placement of the pump catheter.

The surgeon encircles the common hepatic artery to prevent extrahepatic pump perfusion, extending the dissection to the bifurcation of the right and left hepatic arteries. Additional dissection is performed on the right hepatic artery, followed by a mini Cochrane maneuver on the lateral side of the porta hepatis.
The surgeon clips the cystic duct and artery before removing the gallbladder from its fossa to prevent chemical cholecystitis during chemotherapy pump use.

A papaverine-soaked sponge is placed over the gastroduodenal and hepatic arteries. The surgeon creates a pump pocket in the left upper quadrant and inserts the pump catheter through the abdominal wall into the peritoneal cavity. The gastroduodenal artery is ligated.

Bulldog clamps are placed on the common and proper hepatic arteries. An eleven blade is inserted into the abdomen under direct vision, with tension applied to the gastrointestinal artery, to perform a precise arteriotomy.

The surgeon extracts the blade and inserts a vein retractor, followed by the insertion of the pump catheter into the arteriotomy. The catheter is advanced to the junction of the common hepatic artery and the gastroduodenal artery.

Silk ties are applied to secure the catheter at the distal bevel of the pump catheter. After placing the first tie, the surgeon removes the bulldog clamps and flushes heparin saline through the pump catheter to confirm patency. Two additional ties are placed around the pump and gastroduodenal artery, with the catheter being flushed after each tie to ensure it is not occluded.

Blue dye is injected, causing the liver to appear blue. A small area of extrahepatic perfusion is observed in the duodenum, which is corrected by ligating branches anterior to the common bile duct. Further dissection is performed on the right side of the porta hepatis to address the perfusion issue. A subsequent blue dye test shows no dye filling in the duodenum.

An ultrasound of the liver reveals the segment three colorectal liver metastasis. The surgeon marks a resection line on the liver capsule, ensuring a gross margin greater than one centimeter around the metastasis.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to perform parenchymal dissection of the liver. Alternative instruments such as the bipolar and robotic harmonic are also suitable for this dissection.
At the completion of the hepatectomy, the round ligament is ligated. The video shows the pump and the port sites used during the procedure.
